skill.name: writing-hookify-rules
skill.description: Guides users through creating Hookify markdown rules with YAML frontmatter, event matching, regex patterns, and clear trigger messages.
skill.summary: Create Hookify rules that guide Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk command and secret strings, but review shows they are documentation examples for writing detection rules. No executable script, network access, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections come from markdown code fences, inline code, and Hookify event names. They describe rule syntax and do not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is inside markdown examples or explanatory lists. I found no code path that invokes these strings as commands.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Execution Findings Are Regex Examples
low_findings.description: The eval and exec detections appear in patterns meant to match unsafe code in future Hookify rules. They are not called by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is escaped or embedded in regex examples. There is no JavaScript, Python, or shell source that evaluates user input.

low_findings.title: Secret and Sensitive File Findings Are Guardrail Examples
low_findings.description: The API key, .env, credential, and certificate strings are examples for warning about sensitive edits. They do not read or transmit secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The strings appear in examples for matching or warning about sensitive files. I found no filesystem read of .env, key, or certificate files.

low_findings.title: Destructive Command Findings Are Detection Patterns
low_findings.description: The rm, dd, mkfs, chmod, and recursive-delete strings are documented as dangerous commands to detect or block in generated rules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text labels these strings as common patterns and examples. I found no instruction to run the destructive commands.

content.user_title: Write Hookify Rules for AI Workflows
content.value_statement: Hookify rules can be hard to write because they combine markdown, YAML, events, and regex matching. This skill helps create clear local rules that warn, block, or remind AI agents during project work.
actual_capabilities: Explains the markdown and YAML frontmatter structure for Hookify rules.
Helps draft rules for bash, file, stop, prompt, and all events.
Shows how to use simple patterns and multi-condition rule formats.
Provides regex guidance for common command, file, and prompt matches.
Documents file naming, local storage, enabling, disabling, and refinement workflows.
Helps write clear trigger messages with warnings, reasons, and alternatives.
limitations: Does not install Hookify or verify that a project has Hookify configured.
Generated regex patterns still need testing against real project inputs.
Cannot guarantee that rules catch every unsafe command or edit.
Rule behavior depends on the active Hookify and AI tool integration.

use_cases.title: Create Local Safety Rules
use_cases.description: Draft project-local Hookify rules that warn before risky shell commands, sensitive file edits, or debug code changes.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Workflow Checks
use_cases.description: Create stop-event reminders for tests, builds, documentation, and review steps before an AI assistant finishes work.

use_cases.title: Document Guardrail Patterns
use_cases.description: Turn security review requirements into readable Hookify rules with clear messages and targeted match conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Rule
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner rule for one event and one pattern.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hookify rule that warns when a bash command matches this behavior: [describe behavior]. Explain the rule fields briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Add a File Edit Guardrail
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Hookify file-event rule for [file type or path] that detects [text pattern]. Include a message explaining the risk and a safer alternative.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Stop Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Workflow reminder before an agent finishes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hookify stop-event rule that reminds the agent to verify [checks]. Make the message concise and action oriented.

prompt_templates.title: Design Conditional Matching
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Hookify rule with multiple conditions for [event type]. It should match [field one] and [field two], then show a precise warning.

output_examples.input: Warn when production code includes debug logging.
output_examples.output: A Hookify file rule plan with a descriptive name, a targeted file event, a regex for debug logging, and a warning message about removing debug output.
A bash-event rule concept that matches risky deletion patterns and explains why the command should be reviewed before running.

output_examples.input: Remind the agent to run tests before stopping.
output_examples.output: A stop-event checklist message that asks the agent to confirm tests, build status, and documentation updates before completion.

best_practices: Use descriptive rule names that start with verbs such as warn, block, require, or check.
Keep patterns targeted enough to avoid frequent false positives in normal work.
Write trigger messages that explain the issue and give a practical next step.
anti_patterns: Do not use broad patterns that match unrelated words or common file content.
Do not create blocking rules before testing them as warnings first.
Do not rely on one rule to replace code review, tests, or security review.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps write Hookify rule files that guide AI assistants during commands, file edits, prompts, or completion checks.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation and guidance for writing rule files. Command strings in the skill are examples for matching.

faq.question: Where are Hookify rules stored?
faq.answer: The skill describes local rule files under the project .claude directory using the hookify naming pattern.

faq.question: Can it create blocking rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains warn and block actions, but blocking rules should be tested carefully before team use.

faq.question: Does it validate regex automatically?
faq.answer: No. It gives regex guidance, but users should test patterns with their real project inputs.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
